notes.claim: Russia undertook obligations to guarantee Ukraine's sovereignty and territorial integrity under the 1994 Budapest Memorandum
notes.context: In December 1994, Ukraine agreed to relinquish the nuclear arsenal it had inherited from the Soviet Union (then the world's third largest) and join the NPT. In return, Russia, the United States, and the United Kingdom signed a memorandum pledging to respect Ukraine's independence, sovereignty, and territorial integrity. Ukraine and the West regard Russia's 2014 annexation of Crimea and 2022 invasion as serious violations of this memorandum. Russia argues that the 2014 Maidan Revolution changed the legal entity of Ukraine, meaning its obligations do not apply to the current government.
notes.verdict: Official record exists (disagreement over legal effect and applicability)

sources.title: Memorandum on Security Assurances in Connection with Ukraine's Accession to the NPT (Budapest Memorandum)

notes.claim: A referendum was held in Crimea in March 2014 and an annexation to Russia was declared
notes.context: On March 16, 2014, a referendum was held in the Autonomous Republic of Crimea, then under de facto Russian military control, with official results showing 96.77% in favor of joining Russia. Russia considers this a legitimate exercise of the right to self-determination. On March 27, 2014, the UN General Assembly adopted Resolution 68/262 (with 100 countries in favor), reaffirming Ukraine's territorial integrity and declaring the referendum invalid. Most countries other than Russia still do not recognize Russian sovereignty over Crimea.
notes.verdict: Referendum held (rejected as invalid by the majority of the international community)

sources.title: United Nations General Assembly Resolution 68/262: Territorial integrity of Ukraine

notes.claim: The 2014-2015 Minsk Agreements established a ceasefire framework for Donbas, but both sides accused each other of non-compliance
notes.context: Minsk I (September 2014) and Minsk II (February 2015) were ceasefire frameworks agreed upon by Ukraine, Russia, the OSCE, and representatives of the Donetsk/Luhansk People's Republics. Russia claims Ukraine refused the political provisions (granting special status to Donbas; constitutional reforms), while Ukraine argues Russia failed to initiate disarmament and border handover first. Complicating interpretation, French President Macron and former German Chancellor Merkel later suggested the agreements were designed to give Ukraine time to rebuild its military, highlighting the profound gap in how each side understood the accords.
notes.verdict: Agreements exist (disagreement over responsibility for non-compliance)

sources.title: Package of Measures for the Implementation of the Minsk Agreements

notes.claim: It is claimed that at the end of the Cold War, Western nations promised the Soviet Union that NATO would 'not expand one inch eastward'
notes.context: Diplomatic documents record statements made by U.S. Secretary of State James Baker to Soviet President Gorbachev during German reunification negotiations in 1990. Russia cites these as a broken promise and uses them as one basis for justifying the invasion. The U.S. and NATO counter that the remarks were limited to the German context and that no treaty or formal agreement restricting expansion to Central and Eastern Europe was ever concluded. The Helsinki Final Act (1975) enshrines every sovereign state's right to freely choose its alliances, a principle widely held to apply to Ukraine's NATO aspirations.
notes.verdict: Record of statements exists (disagreement over legal bindingness and scope)

notes.claim: Killings of civilians by Russian forces have been confirmed in Bucha and multiple other locations
notes.context: After Russian forces withdrew from areas north of Kyiv in late March 2022, large numbers of civilian bodies were discovered on streets and in buildings in Bucha. The UN Human Rights Monitoring Mission (OHCHR), the International Criminal Court (ICC), and multiple independent investigators documented evidence of extrajudicial killings, torture, and sexual violence against civilians. In March 2023, the ICC issued an arrest warrant for President Putin in connection with the unlawful deportation of children from Ukraine. Russia claims the Bucha footage was staged and does not recognize the ICC's jurisdiction.
notes.verdict: Documented by the UN and ICC (Russia denies and does not recognize ICC jurisdiction)

sources.title: Ukraine: Situation Report (OHCHR)
sources.publisher: UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights

sources.title: ICC Arrest Warrant for Vladimir Putin

notes.claim: In September 2022, Russia declared the 'annexation' of Donetsk, Luhansk, Zaporizhzhia, and Kherson oblasts
notes.context: Referendums were held in Russian-controlled areas from September 23-27, 2022, and on September 30 President Putin declared the annexation of all four oblasts. Official results claimed 87-99% approval, but no international monitors such as the OSCE were present. On October 12, 2022, the UN General Assembly adopted Resolution ES-11/4 (143 countries in favor), declaring these 'illegal referendums' void and calling on Russia to withdraw. Russia has enshrined these four oblasts in its constitution as 'indivisible territory.'
notes.verdict: Annexation declaration is a fact (rejected as invalid by the majority of the international community)

sources.title: United Nations General Assembly Resolution ES-11/4
